markdown:
# EVPulse Block System — Complete Feature Test

This article demonstrates every custom block type available in the EVPulse editor. Each section shows a different element so you can verify rendering on both light and dark themes.

---

## Callouts

[!NOTE]This is a **note** callout — use it for supplementary info that adds context without breaking the flow. Notes appear with a blue left border and light blue background.[/!NOTE]

[!WARNING]This is a **warning** callout — for things that could go wrong. Hardware limits, safety thresholds, irreversible actions. Orange border and background.[/!WARNING]

[!KEY]This is a **key takeaway** — the single most important point from a section. Green border and background to draw attention.[/!KEY]

[!TIP]This is a **tip** callout — practical engineering advice. Things you wish someone had told you earlier. Purple border and background.[/!TIP]

[!DANGER]This is a **danger** callout — critical safety information. Thermal runaway, lithium plating, HV exposure. Red border and background.[/!DANGER]

---

## Pull Quote

[!QUOTE]The BMS is not protecting you from the battery. It is protecting the battery from you — from every aggressive acceleration, every cold-weather fast charge, every week parked at 100% SOC.[/!QUOTE]

---

## Single Stat Card

[!STAT label="Energy Density"]250 Wh/kg[/!STAT]

---

## Stats Row

[!STATS]
Energy Density::250 Wh/kg
Cycle Life::3,000+
Cell Cost::$90/kWh
[/!STATS]

---

## Compare Table

[!COMPARE a="LFP" b="NMC 811"]
✅ Safer — stable olivine structure | ⚠ Higher thermal risk above 200°C
✅ 3,000+ cycle life | ❌ 1,200–1,500 cycles typical
❌ Lower energy density (150 Wh/kg) | ✅ Higher energy density (250 Wh/kg)
❌ Hard SOC estimation in plateau | ✅ Sloped OCV — easier estimation
[/!COMPARE]

---

## Expandable Section

[!EXPAND title="What is the Arrhenius equation and why does it matter?"]The Arrhenius equation describes how reaction rates change with temperature. For lithium-ion cells, it predicts how much slower lithium ions diffuse through the electrolyte at 0°C vs 25°C — typically 3–5× slower. This is the root cause of cold-weather range loss and charging speed reduction.

The equation is: k = A × exp(-Ea / R·T) where k is the rate constant, A is the pre-exponential factor, Ea is activation energy, R is the gas constant, and T is temperature in Kelvin.[/!EXPAND]

---

## Quiz

[!QUIZ q="At what SOC does lithium plating risk become significant during fast charging?" a1="Below 20% SOC" a2="Around 50% SOC" a3="Above 80% SOC" a4="It doesn't depend on SOC" correct=3]At high SOC, the graphite anode is nearly fully lithiated — there is minimal headroom before the anode potential drops below 0V vs Li/Li+, which is the plating threshold. High current at high SOC is the most dangerous combination.[/!QUIZ]

---

## Poll

[!POLL q="How do you primarily charge your EV?"]
Home wallbox overnight|Public AC charger|DC fast charger only|Mix of all three
[/!POLL]

---

## Tabs

[!TABS t1="LFP" t2="NMC" t3="Na-ion"]
**Lithium Iron Phosphate** — the workhorse of commercial EVs. Flat OCV curve makes SOC estimation hard, but the olivine structure gives exceptional thermal stability and cycle life. Dominant in Indian buses and trucks.
---TAB---
**Nickel Manganese Cobalt** — higher energy density, better low-temperature performance, but more complex degradation mechanics and higher thermal runaway risk above 200°C. Dominant in passenger EVs.
---TAB---
**Sodium-ion** — emerging chemistry. No lithium, lower cost, better low-temperature performance than LFP. Energy density still lower than NMC. Hard carbon anode with moderate OCV slope.
[/!TABS]

---

## Named Equations

[!EQ label="Arrhenius Equation"]
k = A \times \exp(-E_a / R \cdot T)
[/!EQ]

[!EQ label="I²R Heat Generation"]
P_{heat} = I^2 \times R_{int}
[/!EQ]

---

## Timeline

[!TIMELINE]
1991::Sony commercialises the first lithium-ion cell — LiCoO₂ cathode, graphite anode
1997::Goodenough and Padhi discover LiFePO₄ — olivine structure, exceptional thermal safety
2008::Tesla Roadster launches — first highway-capable BEV, 53 kWh NCA pack
2020::BYD launches Blade Battery — cell-to-pack LFP, passes nail penetration without fire
2023::Na-ion enters mass production — CATL and HiNa ship first commercial cells
[/!TIMELINE]

---

## Numbered Steps

[!STEPS]
Close negative contactor::Always close negative first — establishes the return path safely before any HV is exposed
Close pre-charge contactor::Series resistor limits inrush current to the DC bus capacitors. Monitor bus voltage rise
Wait for bus voltage > 95%::Capacitors are now charged — closing positive main contactor will cause minimal inrush
Close positive main contactor::System is live. Open pre-charge contactor immediately after
[/!STEPS]

---

## Pros and Cons

[!PROSCONS]
+ Exceptional thermal stability — no runaway below 270°C
+ 3,000+ cycle life at 80% DOD
+ No cobalt — lower cost and ethical sourcing
+ Flat discharge curve — consistent voltage
- Lower energy density — 150 vs 250 Wh/kg for NMC
- Flat OCV makes SOC estimation unreliable
- Poor performance below 0°C — 40% power loss
- Heavier packs for same range
[/!PROSCONS]

---

## Figure with Image Credit

[!FIGURE src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Electric-vehicle-1.jpg/640px-Electric-vehicle-1.jpg" caption="A modern electric vehicle at a public charging station" credit="Wikimedia Commons" creditUrl="https://commons.wikimedia.org"][/!FIGURE]

---

## Inline Highlight

The CC-CV protocol is not a design choice — it is a ==physical constraint of the electrochemistry==. Push current past the constant-voltage threshold and you are not charging faster; you are plating metallic lithium on the anode.

---
